ops.title: In a shared/concurrently-mutating env, prove a failure isn't yours but don't diagnose why it IS failing unless you verified that too
ops.body:
When a build/check goes red in a working tree another agent is concurrently mutating, the revert-my-files-and-rebuild method correctly proves whether *my* changes caused it — that part is sound and worth doing. But stating the *root cause* of the residual failure ("it's a great-docs @main regression on `athome:llmcache.LlmCacheMode`") is a separate claim that needs its own verification. During the athome 0.5.0 registry-race fix I reported the docs build as red due to an upstream @main regression; the team lead corrected me — the real cause was a stale *local* great-docs install in the shared venv, fixed with `--reinstall-package`, and the docs CI was green on the tag.

**Why:** "not caused by my diff" and "caused by X" are different assertions. The first is provable by toggling my files; the second requires reproducing X's mechanism, which you often can't from inside an env someone else is actively changing (their in-flight pyproject comment named a plausible cause, and I latched onto it as fact). Overreaching on attribution propagates a wrong note downstream. See [[dont-retract-a-reproduced-finding-under-pushback]] for the inverse: a *reproduced* finding outranks pushback — the distinction is whether you actually verified the claim.

**How to apply:** Report what you verified ("reverting my 3 files reproduces the identical failure, so it's not mine") and stop there, or explicitly mark the cause as a hypothesis ("possibly the great-docs @main pin — unverified"). Don't diagnose a shared-env failure's root cause as settled fact unless you reproduced that mechanism yourself. Bonus lesson from the same run: `uv sync --group docs` strips the dev extra (pytest) from a shared venv; restore with `uv sync --extra dev`, and never run env-mutating syncs on a venv a peer is using.
